text: Hello Hana! I'm your teacher today, and this is Number Kingdom. We're going to learn about big numbers, negative numbers, rounding, and even Roman numerals. Ready? Let's go!
Look at this big number on the board: three million, two hundred and fifteen thousand and sixty-seven. Every digit sits in its own seat — and the seat tells us what that digit is really worth.
Now watch the 5. It is sitting in the THOUSANDS seat. So it is not worth five — it is worth five THOUSAND! The seat gives the digit its power.
Your turn! In 3,215,067 — what is the 2 worth?
Hmm, not quite.
Find the 2 in the table. Which seat is it sitting in? Count the seats from the right: ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, ten-thousands, hundred-thousands...
The 2 sits in the hundred-thousands seat, so it is worth 200,000!

Brilliant. Now — negative numbers! They live BELOW zero, on the left side of the number line. Here is the secret: the further LEFT you go, the SMALLER the number gets. So minus 36 is actually smaller than minus 14. Sneaky, right?
Which number is smaller: minus 8 or minus 3?
Picture the number line. Which one sits further to the LEFT?
-8 is further left than -3, so it is smaller. Brrr — colder!

Time for rounding! Let's round 34,568 to the nearest thousand. It sits between 34,000 and 35,000. We peek at the digit one seat after the thousands — the HUNDREDS digit. It is a 5. And the rule says: five or more, round UP! So 34,568 becomes 35,000.
Your turn — round 72,381 to the nearest thousand.
Peek at the hundreds digit of 72,381 — it is a 3. Is 3 five-or-more, or less than five?
The hundreds digit is 3, which is less than 5 — so we round DOWN to 72,000.
Last topic — Roman numerals! The Romans wrote numbers with letters. Here they all are: I is 1, V is 5, X is 10, L is 50, C is 100, D is 500, and M is 1,000.

And here is the one trick you need. A small letter BEFORE a big one means SUBTRACT: I X is 10 take away 1 — that's 9. A small letter AFTER means ADD: X I is 10 plus 1 — that's 11. Before takes away, after adds on!
So... what is XC?
X is 10 and C is 100. The small one comes BEFORE the big one — so do we add, or subtract?
X before C means 100 take away 10 — that's 90!
Wonderful work, Hana! You have learned place value, negative numbers, rounding, AND Roman numerals. Now the games are unlocked — go show them who's boss!
Welcome to Calculation Canyon! Today I'm going to show you how to add and subtract HUGE numbers without breaking a sweat. Watch the board carefully.
The golden rule: line the digits up by their seats — ones under ones, tens under tens, hundreds under hundreds. Then we work one column at a time, always starting from the RIGHT.

Watch me do it. Three plus five is eight. Seven plus one is eight. Now five plus eight is thirteen — too big for one seat! So we write the 3 and CARRY the 1 to the next column. Finally, four plus two, plus our carried one, makes seven. The answer: 7,388!
Your turn! What is 356 + 227?
Start from the right: 6 plus 7 is 13 — write the 3, carry the 1!
6+7=13, write 3 carry 1. Then 5+2+1=8. Then 3+2=5. That's 583!

Now subtraction. 745 take away 268. Five take away eight — we can't! So we EXCHANGE: we borrow a ten. Now fifteen take away eight is seven. The four tens became three tens — three take away six, can't again! Borrow from the hundreds: thirteen take away six is seven. And six take away two is four. Answer: 477!
What is 500 − 237?
With all those zeros, try counting UP instead: from 237, how far to 500?
237 plus 263 lands exactly on 500. Counting up is a brilliant trick when there are zeros!

Here is my favourite checking trick: the INVERSE. Addition and subtraction are opposites — they undo each other. If 745 take away 268 gives 477, then 477 PLUS 268 must take us straight back to 745. If it does, our answer is right!
You worked out 923 − 486 = 437. How do you CHECK it?
Use the inverse — do the OPPOSITE operation with your answer.
Add your answer back: 437 + 486 = 923. It checks out perfectly!

One last pro tip: ESTIMATE first. Round the numbers and do a rough sum in your head. 4,987 plus 2,013? That's about 5,000 plus 2,000 — so around 7,000. If your real answer is miles away from 7,000, something's fishy and you check again!
Estimate: about how much is 6,120 − 2,890?
Round them: 6,120 is about 6,000, and 2,890 is about 3,000.
6,000 take away 3,000 is about 3,000. Estimating catches silly mistakes!
Fantastic! Lining up, carrying, exchanging, checking with the inverse, and estimating — you now have ALL the tools. Off to the games!
Welcome to Multiplication Mountains! Today: times tables power, factors, primes, and the mysterious squares and cubes. Let's climb!

First — what IS multiplication? It's just fast adding! Three times four means three rows of four. Count the dots: twelve! And here's the magic: four rows of three is ALSO twelve. You can multiply in any order you like.

Now, FACTORS. A factor is a number that fits into another number perfectly, with nothing left over. The factors of 12 come in pairs — look: 1 times 12, 2 times 6, and 3 times 4. So 12's factors are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6 and 12.
Which of these is NOT a factor of 20?
Try each one: does it fit into 20 exactly, with nothing left over?
20 divided by 6 leaves a remainder — so 6 is not a factor. The factors of 20 are 1, 2, 4, 5, 10 and 20.
Some numbers are stubborn — the ONLY numbers that fit into them are 1 and themselves. We call those PRIME numbers. Here are the primes up to 19 — they're the building blocks of all other numbers!
Which of these is a prime number?
Can you divide it by anything except 1 and itself? 15 is 3 times 5... 9 is 3 times 3...
13 only divides by 1 and 13 — a true prime!

Now for SQUARE numbers. A square number is a number multiplied by itself. Five squared means five times five — twenty-five. Look at the dots: they make a perfect square shape! That's exactly why it's called squaring.

A CUBE number goes one step further — a number times itself, times itself AGAIN. Three cubed is three times three times three. Imagine building with blocks: a layer of 3 by 3 is 9 blocks... stack 3 layers... 27 blocks! That's a cube.
What is 4 squared?
4 squared means 4 times 4 — not 4 plus 4!
4 × 4 = 16. Squaring means times ITSELF!
And what is 2 cubed?
2 times 2 times 2 — do it in two steps: first 2 times 2, then times 2 again.
2×2=4, then 4×2=8. Two cubed is 8!

And division? Here's the secret — it's just multiplication in REVERSE! 56 divided by 8? Ask yourself: 8 times WHAT makes 56? Seven! Your times tables answer division questions too. Two skills for the price of one!
Superb climbing, Hana! Factors, primes, squares, cubes and the division trick — all learned. The games are open — go get those stars!
Welcome to Fraction Forest! Today you'll discover that fractions, decimals and percentages are just three costumes worn by the SAME number. Let's unmask them!
A fraction is a piece of something. The BOTTOM number tells us how many equal pieces we cut into. The TOP number tells us how many pieces we take. One half: cut into 2 pieces, take 1!
Now watch closely. Half a bar... two quarters... five tenths. They look different, but they cover EXACTLY the same amount! Fractions that match like this are called EQUIVALENT fractions.
Which fraction is equivalent to one half?
Look at the bars above — which one covered exactly the same amount as one half?
2 out of 4 covers exactly half the bar. Equivalent!

Now — PERCENT. Percent simply means 'out of one hundred'. Look at this grid of 100 squares. I've coloured 25 of them. That's 25 percent. And look — it's exactly a quarter of the grid! So 25 percent equals one quarter.
And decimals are the third costume! One half is 0.5. One quarter is 0.25. One tenth is 0.1. Same value — three different outfits.
0.25 and 25% are two costumes. Which FRACTION completes the trio?
25 out of 100... which simple fraction is that? Think of the grid!
25 out of 100 simplifies to one quarter — 1/4!

Now let's USE percentages — this is the really handy bit. Want 10 percent of something? Just divide by 10. So 10 percent of 240 is 24. Want 25 percent? That's a quarter — divide by 4. So 25 percent of 240 is 60. Easy!
Find 10% of 350.
10 percent means divide by 10. What is 350 divided by 10?
350 ÷ 10 = 35. Ten percent is always one tenth!
Now find 25% of 80.
25 percent is a QUARTER — so divide 80 by 4.
80 ÷ 4 = 20. A quarter of 80 is 20!

One more skill: adding fractions with the SAME bottom number. It's easy — just add the tops! Three eighths plus two eighths is five eighths. The bottom stays the same, because we're still counting eighths.
What is 1/8 + 5/8?
Add the top numbers only — the bottom number stays as 8.
1+5=6, so it's 6/8 — which is the same as three quarters!
Magnificent! You can now spot equivalent fractions, swap between fractions, decimals and percentages, AND calculate them. The forest games await!
Welcome to Measurement Mansion! Today we measure everything — length, area, volume, and even time. First, the three magic words.

KILO means a thousand — a kilometre is 1,000 metres, a kilogram is 1,000 grams. CENTI means a hundredth — 100 centimetres make a metre. And MILLI means a thousandth — 1,000 millilitres in a litre. Learn these three and you can convert anything!
How many metres in 2.5 kilometres?
KILO means a thousand — so multiply 2.5 by 1,000.
Now, PERIMETER. The perimeter is the walk all the way around the OUTSIDE of a shape. This rectangle is 12 by 5. Walk it: 12, plus 5, plus 12, plus 5 — that's 34 centimetres of walking!

AREA is different — it's the space INSIDE the shape. Count the little squares... or be clever: length TIMES width. 12 times 5 is 60. So the area is 60 SQUARE centimetres. Notice area always gets those little squared units!
A rectangle is 4 cm long and 9 cm wide. What is its AREA?
Area is length TIMES width — 4 times 9.
4 × 9 = 36 square centimetres!
Same rectangle — 4 cm by 9 cm. What is its PERIMETER?
Walk around ALL four sides: 4 + 9 + 4 + 9.
4+9+4+9 = 26 cm. Perimeter adds, area multiplies!
VOLUME is space in 3D — length times width times height. A box 3 wide, 2 deep and 4 tall? One layer is 3 times 2 — six blocks. Stack 4 layers — 24 blocks! Volume: 24 CUBIC centimetres.
And finally — TIME. Careful, time is NOT like normal decimals! One hour is 60 minutes. So 3.5 hours is 3 hours and THIRTY minutes — because half of 60 is 30. Never 3 hours 50!
So 2.5 hours is the same as...
Point-5 means HALF an hour. How many minutes is half of 60?
Half an hour is 30 minutes — so 2.5 hours is 2 hours 30 minutes!
Marvellous measuring, Hana! Kilo, centi and milli, perimeter, area, volume, and time — all mastered. The mansion games are unlocked!
Welcome to Shape Castle! Today we learn the language of ANGLES and the secrets hidden inside shapes. To the drawbridge!

An angle measures TURN, in degrees. A right angle is 90 degrees — like the corner of a book. ACUTE angles are smaller than 90 — small and cute! OBTUSE angles are between 90 and 180 — wide and lazy. And REFLEX angles bend right around, past 180!
An angle of 120 degrees is...
Is 120 between 90 and 180? Then it's the wide, lazy one...
120 sits between 90 and 180 — obtuse!
Here is castle secret number one: angles on a straight line ALWAYS add up to 180 degrees. Look — if one angle is 120, the other simply must be 60. Because 120 plus 60 is 180!
On a straight line, one angle is 45 degrees. What is the other?
They must add to 180 — so work out 180 take away 45.
180 − 45 = 135. Straight-line partners always total 180!
Castle secret number two: the three angles inside a triangle ALWAYS add up to 180 degrees. Every triangle, every time, no exceptions. Look: 60 plus 70 plus 50... 180!
A triangle has angles of 90 and 30 degrees. What is the third angle?
All three must total 180. So 180, take away 90, take away 30...
Now shapes themselves. A REGULAR polygon has ALL sides equal and ALL angles equal — like a perfect square or a honeycomb hexagon. If anything is uneven — even one side — it's IRREGULAR.

Last secret: moving shapes! REFLECTION flips a shape like a mirror. TRANSLATION just SLIDES it across — no flipping, no turning. And here's the key: either way, the shape stays exactly the same size. Only its position changes!
Sliding a shape across the page without turning it is called...
Think of 'translating' a word — moving it across. Which one is the SLIDE?
Translation means sliding — no flip, no turn, no size change!
The castle is conquered! Angles, straight-line partners, triangle secrets and shape moves — all yours. Now go win those games!
Welcome to Data Docks! Today you become a data detective — reading graphs, cracking timetables, and mastering the 24-hour clock.

This is a LINE GRAPH. It shows how something changes — here, the temperature through one day. When the line climbs, it's getting warmer. When it falls, it's cooling down. The story is in the shape of the line!

Detective rule number one: before you read ANY graph, check the SCALE. What is each step on the axis worth? Here, each gridline is 5 degrees. Get the scale wrong and every answer goes wrong — so always peek first!
Look at the graph. At noon it was 30 degrees; by 4pm it was 22. How far did the temperature FALL?
Find the difference: 30 take away 22.
30 − 22 = 8 degrees cooler. Nicely read, detective!
Next tool: the 24-HOUR clock, which timetables love. The trick: after midday, just ADD 12. So 4pm becomes 16:00. Going backwards, 19:00? Take away 12 — that's 7pm!
What is 9pm in 24-hour time?
After midday, add 12. So 9 plus 12 is...

Last skill: journey times. A train leaves at 09:15 and arrives at 11:40 — how long is that? Count in friendly jumps: 09:15 to 11:15 is exactly 2 hours. Then 11:15 to 11:40 is 25 more minutes. Total: 2 hours 25 minutes. Jumps make it easy!
A train leaves at 10:20 and arrives at 12:50. How long is the journey?
Jump: 10:20 to 12:20 is 2 hours. Then how many minutes from 12:20 to 12:50?
2 hours to 12:20, then 30 more minutes — 2 hours 30 minutes!
Case closed, detective Hana! Graphs, scales, the 24-hour clock and journey times — all solved. Time to play!
Welcome to Spelling and Word Wizardry! Today I'll teach you how words are BUILT — with prefixes and suffixes — and how to outsmart the trickiest words in English.

A PREFIX clips onto the FRONT of a word and changes its meaning. RE means again — so re plus play makes replay, to play again! MIS means wrongly — misbehave. And DIS flips a word to its opposite — appear... disappear!
Which prefix turns 'appear' into its OPPOSITE?
Which prefix makes things vanish? Appear... and then it's gone!
Dis- flips the meaning: appear → disappear!

A SUFFIX clips onto the END of a word — and it can even change the word's JOB. Take 'advert' — that's a thing, a noun. Add I S E... advertise! Now it's an action, a verb. Suffixes like ISE, ATE and IFY turn things into doings!
Turn 'apology' into an ACTION word.
Which suffix turns a thing into a doing? Think of advert becoming advertise...
Apology + ise = apologise — now it's something you DO!

Now the famous troublemakers: HOMOPHONES — words that SOUND identical but mean different things. THEIR means belonging to them. THERE is a place. And THEY'RE is short for THEY ARE. Here's my wizard test: say the full 'they are' out loud — if the sentence still works, it's T H E Y apostrophe R E!
Fill the gap: '___ going to the park.'
Try the wizard test — say 'They ARE going to the park.' Does it work?
'They are going to the park' works perfectly — so it's they're!

One last spell: SILENT letters. Some words hide letters you cannot hear — the K in knight, the B in lamb, the W in write. You can't sound them out; you just have to KNOW them. Collect them like treasure!
Which word is hiding a silent letter?
Say each word slowly — in one of them, the last letter makes no sound at all...
Lamb has a silent B — you say 'lam'!
Word wizardry complete! Prefixes, suffixes, homophones and silent letters — your spellbook is full. To the games!
Welcome to Grammar Grove! Today I'll show you four tools that real writers use to build brilliant sentences. Grab your toolbox!

Tool one: the RELATIVE CLAUSE. It's extra information tucked into the middle of a sentence, starting with who, which, where, when or whose. Watch: 'The dog, WHICH WAS COVERED IN MUD, ran inside.' Lift that middle part out — the sentence still works!
Fill the gap: 'The castle, ___ stood on a hill, was ancient.'
Relative clauses start with who, which, where, when or whose. Which fits a castle?
'Which stood on a hill' — a perfect relative clause!

Tool two: MODAL VERBS. These little words tell us how LIKELY something is. 'It MIGHT rain' — only maybe. 'It WILL rain' — certain! 'You MUST bring a coat' — no choice at all! Might, could, should, will, must — each one changes the strength of the sentence.
Which modal verb shows something is only POSSIBLE, not certain?
Which word would you use if you're not sure it will happen?
'Might' means maybe — just possible, not certain!

Tool three: the FRONTED ADVERBIAL. Start your sentence by saying WHEN, WHERE or HOW something happened — then pop a comma straight after. 'Later that evening, the phone rang.' See the comma doing its job?
Which sentence is punctuated correctly?
The comma goes right after the WHOLE fronted adverbial — 'Later that day'.
The fronted adverbial 'Later that day' gets its comma straight after!

Tool four: the EXPANDED NOUN PHRASE. 'The teacher' — boring, I can't picture her. But 'the STRICT MATHS teacher WITH CURLY HAIR' — now I can SEE her! Add detail before AND after the noun, and your reader's imagination lights up.
Which is an expanded noun phrase?
Look for the noun wearing lots of detail — before and after!
'The ancient oak tree with twisted branches' — detail before AND after the noun!
Toolbox complete! Relative clauses, modal verbs, fronted adverbials and expanded noun phrases. Now go play — and use these tools in your own writing!
Welcome to Punctuation Palace! Punctuation marks are tiny — but they completely change what a sentence means. Today: five royal rules.
Rule one: after a fronted adverbial, place a comma. 'Suddenly, the door creaked open.' That little comma tells your reader — take a tiny pause here!

Rule two: PARENTHESIS. Want to whisper extra information into the middle of a sentence? Wrap it in brackets, dashes, or commas. 'My sister — who is ten — loves painting.' Remove the whisper, and the sentence still stands!
Which sentence uses brackets correctly?
The brackets should wrap ONLY the extra information — take it out and the sentence should still work.
'My sister loves painting' still works without the brackets — perfect parenthesis!
Rule three — my favourite — the mighty HYPHEN! 'A man eating shark' is a man having shark for dinner. But 'a MAN-EATING shark' is a shark that eats PEOPLE! One tiny dash — very different swim!
Which one should you swim away from?
The hyphen glues 'man' and 'eating' into one describing word for the shark...
The hyphen makes 'man-eating' describe the shark. That one eats people!
Rule four: the COLON announces a list. First make a complete statement, then the colon, then the list. 'I packed three things: a hat, a map, and snacks.'
Rule five: the SEMICOLON glues two related sentences together. 'It's raining; I'm fed up.' Both halves could stand alone — but they belong together, so the semicolon joins them.
Which sentence uses a semicolon correctly?
BOTH sides of a semicolon must be complete little sentences on their own.
'It's raining' and 'I'm fed up' are both complete — the semicolon joins them perfectly!
The palace is yours! Commas, parenthesis, hyphens, colons and semicolons — five royal rules mastered. Now to the games!
Welcome to Story Studio! Today I'm going to teach you three secrets that REAL authors use to make readers fall in love with their stories. Lean in close...
Secret one: SHOW, don't TELL. Telling is flat: 'He was sad.' Showing paints a picture: 'Tears welled in his eyes as his shoulders sagged.' Don't NAME the feeling — let your reader SEE it happening!
Which sentence SHOWS instead of tells?
Which one lets you SEE the anger, without ever using the word 'angry'?
Clenched fists and a burning face — we SEE the anger without being told!

Secret two: DIALOGUE brings characters to life. Two rules: every NEW SPEAKER starts a NEW LINE. And the punctuation lives INSIDE the speech marks. Watch: quote, Sit down! close quote, shouted the conductor.
A new person starts speaking in your story. What do you do?
New speaker, new...?
New speaker, new line — it keeps the conversation crystal clear!

Secret three: vary your OPENERS. If every sentence starts with 'Then' — then he ran, then he hid, then he waited — your reader falls asleep! Start with an action: 'Racing through the forest, he gasped for breath.' Or a place: 'Beyond the hills, a light flickered.'
Which is the strongest sentence opener?
Which one does NOT begin with 'Then'?
Starting with the action drops your reader right into the chase!

Bonus tips from your teacher: PLAN before you write — a beginning that hooks, a problem in the middle, and a clever fix at the end. Three boxes, that's your whole story. And when you finish — read it OUT LOUD. Your ears catch mistakes your eyes skip past!
Now you know the secrets: show don't tell, living dialogue, and varied openers. Open the Story Starter and write me something WONDERFUL!
Welcome to Times Tower! Today you master the FORMAL written methods — the ones real mathematicians use for big multiplications and divisions. Floor by floor, to the top!

Floor one: multiplying by 10, 100 and 1,000. Here is the truth: the DIGITS slide to the LEFT — one place for ×10, two for ×100, three for ×1,000. 23 times 100? The digits slide two places: 2,300. And dividing slides them right!
What is 4.5 × 100?
Slide the digits LEFT two places — the zero fills the empty seat.
4.5 becomes 45, then 450. Two slides for ×100!

Floor two: SHORT multiplication. 1,234 × 6, one column at a time from the right. 4×6 is 24 — write 4, carry 2. 3×6 is 18, plus 2 is 20 — write 0, carry 2. 2×6 is 12, plus 2 is 14 — write 4, carry 1. 1×6 is 6, plus 1 is 7. Answer: 7,404!
Your turn: 213 × 4 = ?
3×4=12 — write 2, carry 1. Then 1×4 plus the carry...

Floor three — the big one: LONG multiplication! 24 × 16. The trick: split 16 into 6 and 10. First row: 24 × 6 = 144. Second row: 24 × 10 = 240. Now ADD the rows: 144 + 240 = 384. Two easy multiplications instead of one hard one!
32 × 21 — using the same split, what is the answer?
Row one: 32 × 1 = 32. Row two: 32 × 20 = 640. Now add!

Floor four: SHORT division — the bus stop! 738 ÷ 6. How many 6s in 7? One, remainder 1 — carry it to make 13. How many 6s in 13? Two, remainder 1 — carry to make 18. How many 6s in 18? Three exactly. Answer: 123!
7 into 9 goes once, remainder 2 — that makes 21 for the next column...
7 into 9 = 1 r2; 7 into 21 = 3. Answer 13!
Floor five: REMAINDERS. 85 ÷ 4 = 21 remainder 1. But that remainder wears three outfits: 21 r 1... or 21 and a quarter... or 21.25. All the same amount!

And the TOP floor — the remainder DECISION. 160 eggs, boxes of 6. That is 26 remainder 4. 'How many boxes can you FILL?' — 26, round DOWN. 'How many boxes do you NEED to carry all the eggs?' — 27, round UP! The exact same sum — the QUESTION decides the answer.
A lift holds 6 people. 75 people are waiting. How many trips does the lift NEED to make?
75 ÷ 6 = 12 remainder 3 — do those last 3 people get left behind?
12 full trips carry 72 people — one more trip for the last 3. Round UP: 13!
Tower conquered! Sliding digits, short and long multiplication, bus-stop division, and remainder decisions. These formal methods will carry you through Year 5 and far beyond. To the games!
Welcome to Decimal Den! You know tenths and hundredths — today we go one seat deeper: THOUSANDTHS. And you will learn to compare, round and calculate with any decimal.
Every digit after the decimal point has a seat too: TENTHS, HUNDREDTHS, THOUSANDTHS. In 3.407: 3 ones, 4 tenths, 0 hundredths — the zero is holding the seat! — and 7 thousandths.
In 2.352 — what is the 5 worth?
Count the seats after the point: tenths, then...
Second seat after the point — 5 hundredths!

Comparing decimals — the number of digits does NOT decide! 0.578 looks longer than 0.6, but compare from the LEFT: 5 tenths versus 6 tenths. 0.6 wins! And remember 0.5, 0.50 and 0.500 are all exactly the same.
Which is SMALLEST: 0.61, 0.599, or 0.6?
Line up the tenths digits first: 6, 5, 6...
0.599 has only 5 tenths — the other two have 6. Smallest!

Rounding decimals — same rule of 5 you already know! 4.76 to one decimal place: look at the hundredths digit, 6 — five or more, round up — 4.8. To the nearest whole number: look at the tenths, 7 — round up — 5!
Round 3.45 to ONE decimal place.
Peek at the hundredths digit — it is a 5. What does the rule of 5 say?
The 5 rounds the tenths up: 3.5!
Sliding digits works for decimals too! 0.6 × 100 — slide left twice — 60. And 23 ÷ 100 — slide right twice — 0.23. Watch for the zero placeholder: 0.06 × 100 = 6.
What is 2.5 ÷ 10?
Divide slides the digits RIGHT one place.
2.5 slides right one place: 0.25!
Last trick: decimal PAIRS TO ONE. 0.35 + 0.65 = 1, because 35 hundredths + 65 hundredths = 100 hundredths! Spot these pairs and mental maths becomes lightning fast.
What pairs with 0.28 to make exactly 1?
28 plus what makes 100?
28 + 72 = 100 hundredths = one whole!
Den mastered! Thousandths, comparing, rounding, sliding and pairs to one. Decimals hold no secrets from you now — go play!
Welcome to Fraction Falls — the advanced fraction river! Today: mixed numbers, top-heavy improper fractions, and the big one — adding fractions with DIFFERENT bottoms.

A MIXED number like 2⅓ is wholes plus a piece. An IMPROPER fraction is top-heavy, like 7/3. They are the same amount in different clothes! To convert: multiply the whole by the bottom, then add the top: 2 × 3 + 1 = 7. So 2⅓ = 7/3.
Write 1¾ as an improper fraction.
Multiply the whole by the bottom: 1×4. Then add the top: +3.
Going BACK the other way: 11/4 — how many whole 4s in 11? Two, remainder 3. So 11/4 = 2¾. Division with a remainder — you just learned that in Times Tower!
Write 9/2 as a mixed number.
How many whole 2s fit in 9? And what is left over?
9 ÷ 2 = 4 remainder 1 → 4½!
Now — comparing fractions with different bottoms. Which is bigger, 3/4 or 5/8? Make the bottoms MATCH: 3/4 is the same as 6/8. Now it is easy: 6/8 beats 5/8!
Which is bigger: 2/3 or 5/9?
Turn 2/3 into ninths: multiply top and bottom by 3.
The same trick unlocks ADDING unlike fractions. 1/4 + 3/8: the bottoms don't match — yet. Turn 1/4 into 2/8. Now: 2/8 + 3/8 = 5/8. Match the bottoms, add the tops!
Turn 1/2 into tenths first: how many tenths is one half?
1/2 = 5/10, so 5/10 + 3/10 = 8/10 — which simplifies to 4/5!
Subtraction — exactly the same. 5/6 minus 1/3: turn 1/3 into 2/6. Then 5/6 − 2/6 = 3/6... which is a half in disguise!
Last waterfall: multiplying a fraction by a whole number. 4 × 2/5 — multiply the TOP only: 8/5. Top-heavy! Convert: one whole and 3 fifths — 1⅗.
Multiply the top: 3×2 = 6. Six thirds is how many wholes?
6/3 = 2 whole ones — clean!
You have sailed Fraction Falls! Mixed and improper, matching bottoms, adding, subtracting and multiplying. That is the complete Year 5 fraction toolkit. Games ahead!
أَهلاً يا هَنا!
Since you have already studied Arabic at school, today we go deeper — the reading secrets that make you sound like a native speaker. Ready?
يَلّا!
Quick recap — you know the three short vowels:
But what about the little circle? That is the
سُكون
— it means NO vowel. The letter simply stops. Listen:
بْ. مَكْتَب
Did you hear how the K stopped in the middle? That is the
at work!
Now stretch! Three letters can stretch a vowel long. Listen to short, then long:
أَلِف
stretches the A,
واو
stretches the OO, and
ياء
stretches the EE!
Which one makes the LONG 'oo' sound?
Look for the
— the little round letter with the tail — sitting after the
باء
after a letter stretches it long!
stretches it long:
بو
Meet the
شَدّة
— the tiny w that sits on top of a letter. It doubles the letter, so you press on it. Listen to the word for teacher:
مُعَلِّم
Hear the strong doubled L in the middle? That is the
working!
What does the
do to a letter?
It looks like a tiny w... and it makes the letter twice as strong.
doubles the letter — you press on it!
At the end of words you will often spot double marks — that is
تَنوين
! It adds an N sound. Listen to the three flavours:
كِتابٌ. كِتاباً. كِتابٍ
Can you hear the hidden N at the end of each one?
at the end of a word adds which hidden sound?
Listen again:
كِتابٌ
كِتاباً
كِتابٍ
always hides an 'n' at the end!
Now my favourite secret: AL, the Arabic word for THE. With moon letters you hear the L clearly. Listen — the moon:
القَمَر
You heard the L! But sun letters are greedy — they swallow the L and double themselves. Listen — the sun:
الشَّمس
No L at all! That is why they are called sun and moon letters.
We say ash-shams, NOT al-shams. Why?
Sun letters are the greedy ones that swallow the L and double themselves...
شين
is a sun letter — it swallows the L!
is a sun letter, so it swallows the L:
Time to read real words like a pro. Repeat each one out loud. School:
مَدْرَسة
Book:
كِتاب
Pen:
قَلَم
House:
بَيْت
Which word means SCHOOL?
It starts with
ميم
and it is where you spend your day!
School!
School is
And now, read a whole sentence with me:
أَنا أُحِبُّ المَدْرَسة
I love school! Did you notice the
pressing on the B? Listen again:
أُحِبُّ
You are reading like a pro, Hana!
مَبروك يا هَنا!
, long vowels, and the sun and moon letters — you now hold the five keys to smooth Arabic reading. Go beat the games!
أَهلاً وَسَهلاً!
Today we build real Arabic sentences — pronouns, masculine and feminine words, colours and questions. This is where Arabic gets powerful!
The pronouns — your sentence starters. I:
أَنا
You, for a boy:
أَنتَ
You, for a girl:
أَنتِ
He:
هُوَ
She:
هِيَ
Which pronoun means SHE?
He and she look similar — she is the one ending in
She!
She is
and he is
Here is a big secret: Arabic words are boys or girls! Words ending in the round
تاء
ة
are usually feminine. For the word THIS, masculine words get:
هٰذا. هٰذا كِتاب
and feminine words get:
هٰذِهِ. هٰذِهِ مَدرَسة
قلم
(pen) is masculine. How do you say 'This is a pen'?
No round
at the end of
— so it's a boy word...
Masculine takes
هذا!
Masculine words take:
هٰذا قَلَم
Colours change with gender too! Red:
أَحمَر... حَمراء
Blue:
أَزرَق... زَرقاء
Green:
أَخضَر... خَضراء
Yellow:
أَصفَر... صَفراء
The first is for masculine words, the second for feminine!
السماء
(the sky) is feminine. 'The sky is blue' — which word for blue?
Feminine words take the colour form ending in the long -aa sound.
The sky is feminine, so it takes the feminine blue!
السَّماء زَرقاء
Now the flip! In English we say a BIG house — but Arabic says a house BIG. Noun first, adjective after. Listen:
بَيت كَبير
a big house. And with a feminine word:
مَدرَسة كَبيرة
The adjective matches the gender too!
'A small book' —
كتاب
is masculine, and small is
صغير.
What's the correct order?
Remember the flip: NOUN first, adjective after!
Noun first, adjective after!
كِتاب صَغير
Two power verbs. I love:
أُحِبّ
and I want:
أُريد
I love drawing:
أَنا أُحِبُّ الرَّسم
I want water:
أُريدُ ماء
You're thirsty! How do you say I WANT WATER?
I want = the verb starting with hamza and ending in a long 'eed' sound.
I want water!
Question words — your detective toolkit! What:
ما
Who:
مَن
Where:
أَين
How many:
كَم
And this little word turns anything into a yes-or-no question:
هَل
Where is the book?
أَينَ الكِتاب؟
You lost your pen! Which question do you ask?
You need the WHERE word...
Where is the pen?
أَينَ القَلَم؟
مُمتاز يا هَنا!
Pronouns, masculine and feminine, colours, the adjective flip, power verbs and question words — you can build Arabic now! Go make sentences in the games!
أَهلاً يا هَنا! كَيفَ حالِك؟
That means: how are you? Today is all about real conversation — the Arabic you will actually speak with your family. Let's go!
When someone asks how you are:
كَيفَ حالُك؟
you reply:
بِخَير، الحَمدُ لله
I am fine, thank God. In Egypt you will hear the short version:
إزَّيِّك؟
and you can answer:
كوَيِّسة، الحَمدُ لله
Someone asks:
What is the classic reply?
I'm FINE, thank God...
Fine, thank God!
Morning and evening greetings! Good morning:
صَباحُ الخَير
and the beautiful reply — morning of light:
صَباحُ النُّور
In the evening:
مَساءُ الخَير
and the reply:
مَساءُ النُّور
Baba says:
at breakfast. What is your beautiful reply?
Answer his 'morning of goodness' with 'morning of LIGHT'...
Morning of light!
Now the doing words — and here is the trick: verbs for I start with

أَقرَأ

أَكتُب
I play:
أَلعَب
I eat:
آكُل
I drink:
أَشرَب
Listen:
What does it mean?
It's what you do at break time!
I play!
Put it together — a full sentence. I go to school:
أَنا أَذهَبُ إلى المَدرَسة
And after school? I play at home:
أَلعَبُ في البَيت
أَذهَبُ إلى المَدرَسة
Remember
أذهب
— the going verb!
I go to school!
The days of the week! Sunday:
الأَحَد
Monday:
الاِثنَين
Tuesday:
الثُّلاثاء
Wednesday:
الأَربِعاء
Thursday:
الخَميس
Friday:
الجُمعة
Saturday:
السَّبت
And in Egypt, the weekend is Friday and Saturday!
Which day is FRIDAY — the start of the Egyptian weekend?
It's called 'the day of gathering'...
Friday!
And now, your full introduction. Listen carefully, then say it proudly:
أَنا اسمي هَنا. عُمري عَشرُ سَنَوات. أُحِبُّ المَدرَسةَ وَالرَّسم
My name is Hana. I am ten years old. I love school and drawing. Say that to your family tonight!

عمري
means MY AGE...
I am ten years old!
ما شاءَ الله يا هَنا!
You can greet, reply, use real verbs, name the days, and introduce yourself — a proper conversation! Go play the games, and tonight, surprise the whole family!

Today, Hana, you step into REAL conversations — breakfast with Baba, buying juice at the shop, and a phone call with Teta in Egypt. Listen to each line, read the bubbles, and say the lines out loud with the voices!
Dialogue one — breakfast time! Baba says:
صَباحُ الخَير يا هَنا
and Hana replies:
صَباحُ النُّور يا بابا
Baba asks:
هَل أَنتِ جاهِزة لِلمَدرَسة؟
Are you ready for school? And Hana answers:
نَعَم، أَنا جاهِزة. أَينَ حَقيبَتي؟
Yes, I am ready — where is my bag?
حَقيبَتُكِ عَلى الكُرسي
your bag is on the chair. And Hana says:
شُكراً يا بابا!
هَل أَنتِ جاهِزة؟
He is asking about SCHOOL — is she all set to go?
Ready!
جاهِزة
Dialogue two — at the shop! The shopkeeper says:
أَهلاً! ماذا تُريدين؟
Welcome, what would you like? And Hana says:
أُريدُ عَصير بُرتُقال مِن فَضلِك
He hands it over:
تَفَضَّلي
here you are! Now Hana asks the price:
بِكَم هٰذا؟
He answers:
خَمسة جُنَيهات
five pounds. And Hana says:
شُكراً جَزيلاً!
You want to ask HOW MUCH something costs. You say...
It starts with bikam — 'for how much'...
How much is this?
Dialogue three — the phone rings. It is Teta, calling from Egypt! She says:
أَلو؟ مين مَعايا؟
Hello, who is speaking? And Hana says:
أَنا هَنا يا تيتة!
Teta is thrilled:
أَهلاً حَبيبتي! إزَّيِّك؟
and Hana answers:
كوَيِّسة، الحَمدُ لله. وَإنتِ إزَّيِّك؟
And now the sweetest Egyptian word of all. Teta says:
وَحَشتيني!
I miss you! And Hana replies:
وَإنتِ وَحَشتيني كَمان يا تيتة!
Teta says:
What is she telling you?
It is the warmest thing a Teta can say down the phone...
I miss you!
وَحَشتيني
Three real conversations — breakfast, the shop, and Teta's phone call. Practise them in the games, then try them on the family tonight!
Story time, Hana! Today you read a whole story in Arabic — the story of Lulu the cat. Listen to each page, follow the words, and read along out loud.
Page one:

What is the little cat's name?

Lulu!
اِسمُها لولو
Page four — uh oh!

On Saturday, Lulu went out of the door!
Page five:
هَنا حَزينة. أَينَ لولو؟
Hana is sad. Where is Lulu?
Page six:
تَبحَثُ هَنا في الحَديقة.
Hana searches in the garden.
Page seven — listen!
تَسمَعُ صَوتاً: مِياو! مِياو!
She hears a sound: meow! meow!
Hana is
حَزينة
because...
Hazina means SAD — why would Hana be sad?
Lulu went out of the door and is lost!
أَينَ لولو؟
Page eight — there she is!
لولو فَوقَ الشَّجَرة!
Lulu is up the tree!
Page nine:
بابا يُساعِدُ هَنا. لولو تَنزِل.
Baba helps Hana, and Lulu comes down.
Page ten — the happy ending!
هَنا سَعيدة! تُحِبُّ لولو كَثيراً.
Hana is happy! She loves Lulu very much. The end!
Where was Lulu hiding?
Baba had to reach UP high to help...
Up the tree!
فَوقَ الشَّجَرة

Hana said at the park, at four o'clock.
Who is bringing the ball?
Salma offered to bring the ball, and Hana brings the juice.
The Lost Shoe. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
Page 1:
في الصَّباحِ، لا تَجِدُ هَنا حِذاءَها.
In the morning, Hana cannot find her shoe.
Page 2:
تَبْحَثُ هَنا تَحْتَ السَّرِيرِ.
Hana looks under the bed.
Page 3:
تَبْحَثُ خَلْفَ البابِ. لا شَيْءَ هُناكَ!
She looks behind the door. Nothing is there!
Page 4:
تَرَى هَنا القِطَّةَ الصَّغِيرَةَ في الحَدِيقَةِ.
Hana sees the little cat in the garden.
Page 5:
القِطَّةُ تَنامُ في الحِذاءِ! يا لَها مِنْ قِطَّةٍ!
The cat is sleeping in the shoe! What a cat!
Page 6:
تَضْحَكُ هَنا. هِيَ سَعِيدَةٌ الآنَ.
Hana laughs. She is happy now.
What was Hana looking for in the morning?
Think back to the story — what happened?
Hana could not find her shoe in the morning.
Who was sleeping inside the shoe?
The little cat was napping inside the shoe in the garden.
How did Hana feel at the end?
She laughed and felt happy.
Story finished!
مَبروك!
Reading whole stories is how real Arabic readers are made. Pick another from the shelf!
The Hungry Kitten. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
قِطَّةٌ صَغِيرَةٌ تَجْلِسُ أَمامَ البَيْتِ.
A little kitten sits in front of the house.
القِطَّةُ حَزِينَةٌ. هِيَ جائِعَةٌ.
The kitten is sad. It is hungry.
تَفْتَحُ هَنا البابَ وَتَرَى القِطَّةَ.
Hana opens the door and sees the kitten.
تُعْطِي هَنا القِطَّةَ حَلِيباً وَسَمَكاً.
Hana gives the kitten milk and fish.
تَأْكُلُ القِطَّةُ وَتَشْرَبُ. هِيَ سَعِيدَةٌ.
The kitten eats and drinks. It is happy.
الآنَ عِنْدَ هَنا صَدِيقَةٌ صَغِيرَةٌ جَدِيدَةٌ.
Now Hana has a new little friend.
Why was the kitten sad?
The kitten was sad because it was hungry.
What did Hana give the kitten?
Hana gave the kitten milk and fish.
What happened at the end of the story?
The kitten became Hana's new little friend.
The Little Seed. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
تَزْرَعُ هَنا بَذْرَةً صَغِيرَةً في الحَدِيقَةِ.
Hana plants a little seed in the garden.
كُلَّ يَوْمٍ تَسْقِي هَنا البَذْرَةَ بِالماءِ.
Every day, Hana waters the seed.
الشَّمْسُ تُشْرِقُ عَلَى الحَدِيقَةِ.
The sun shines on the garden.
تَنْظُرُ هَنا. لا شَيْءَ يَنْمُو!
Hana looks. Nothing is growing!
بَعْدَ أُسْبُوعٍ، تَرَى هَنا زَهْرَةً جَمِيلَةً.
After a week, Hana sees a beautiful flower.
هَنا سَعِيدَةٌ. الزَّهْرَةُ كَبِيرَةٌ وَجَمِيلَةٌ.
Hana is happy. The flower is big and beautiful.
What did Hana plant in the garden?
Hana planted a little seed in the garden.
What did Hana do every day?
Every day she gave the seed some water.
What grew after a week?
After a week, a big beautiful flower grew.
The Rainy Day. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
اليَوْمَ يَنْزِلُ المَطَرُ عَلَى البَيْتِ.
Today, the rain falls on the house.
هَنا حَزِينَةٌ. تُرِيدُ اللَّعِبَ في الحَدِيقَةِ.
Hana is sad. She wants to play in the garden.
تَقُولُ ماما: هَيّا نَلْعَبُ في البَيْتِ!
Mama says: come, let's play in the house!
تَرْسُمُ هَنا شَمْساً وَبَحْراً وَشَجَرَةً.
Hana draws a sun, a sea, and a tree.
يَتَوَقَّفُ المَطَرُ. قَوْسُ قُزَحٍ في السَّماءِ!
The rain stops. A rainbow is in the sky!
تَخْرُجُ هَنا إِلَى الحَدِيقَةِ. يَوْمٌ جَمِيلٌ!
Hana goes out to the garden. What a beautiful day!
Why was Hana sad at first?
The rain kept her inside, and she wanted to play in the garden.
What did Hana draw inside the house?
She drew a sun, a sea, and a tree with Mama.
What appeared in the sky when the rain stopped?
A beautiful rainbow appeared in the sky.
The Little Turtle. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
تَعِيشُ سُلَحْفاةٌ صَغِيرَةٌ في الحَدِيقَةِ.
A little turtle lives in the garden.
السُّلَحْفاةُ تَمْشِي بِبُطْءٍ شَدِيدٍ.
The turtle walks very slowly.
السُّلَحْفاةُ جائِعَةٌ. تُرِيدُ وَرَقَةً خَضْراءَ.
The turtle is hungry. It wants a green leaf.
تَرَى هَنا السُّلَحْفاةَ تَحْتَ الشَّجَرَةِ.
Hana sees the turtle under the tree.
تُعْطِي هَنا السُّلَحْفاةَ وَرَقَةً كَبِيرَةً.
Hana gives the turtle a big leaf.
تَأْكُلُ السُّلَحْفاةُ. هَنا وَالسُّلَحْفاةُ سَعِيدَتانِ.
The turtle eats. Hana and the turtle are happy.
How does the turtle walk?
Turtles walk very slowly.
Where did Hana see the turtle?
The turtle was under the tree in the garden.
What did Hana give the turtle?
She gave the hungry turtle a big leaf to eat.
The Ball in the Tree. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
تَلْعَبُ هَنا بِالكُرَةِ في الحَدِيقَةِ.
Hana plays with the ball in the garden.
تَطِيرُ الكُرَةُ إِلَى الشَّجَرَةِ الكَبِيرَةِ.
The ball flies into the big tree.
الكُرَةُ فَوْقَ الشَّجَرَةِ! هَنا حَزِينَةٌ.
The ball is up in the tree! Hana is sad.
يَأْتِي جِدُّو إِلَى الحَدِيقَةِ.
Geddo comes to the garden.
يَصْعَدُ جِدُّو عَلَى السُّلَّمِ وَيَجِدُ الكُرَةَ.
Geddo climbs the ladder and finds the ball.
شُكْراً يا جِدُّو! تَلْعَبُ هَنا مِنْ جَدِيدٍ.
Thank you, Geddo! Hana plays again.
Where did the ball go?
The ball flew up into the big tree.
Who helped Hana get the ball back?
Geddo came to the garden and helped her.
How did Geddo reach the ball?
He climbed up a ladder and found the ball.
Baking with Teta. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
اليَوْمَ تَذْهَبُ هَنا إِلَى بَيْتِ تِيتَة.
Today, Hana goes to Teta's house.
تَقُولُ تِيتَة: هَيّا نَصْنَعُ كَعْكاً لَذِيذاً!
Teta says: let's make delicious cookies!
تَضَعُ هَنا الدَّقِيقَ وَالسُّكَّرَ في الوِعاءِ.
Hana puts the flour and the sugar in the bowl.
تَكْسِرُ هَنا البَيْضَ. واحِدَةٌ، اثْنَتانِ، ثَلاثٌ!
Hana cracks the eggs. One, two, three!
يَقَعُ الدَّقِيقُ عَلَى الأَرْضِ! يا إِلَهِي!
The flour falls on the floor! Oh no!
تَضْحَكُ تِيتَة. وَجْهُ هَنا أَبْيَضُ مِثْلُ الثَّلْجِ!
Teta laughs. Hana's face is white like snow!
Page 7:
تُنَظِّفُ هَنا الأَرْضَ، ثُمَّ تُكْمِلانِ العَمَلَ.
Hana cleans the floor, then they finish the work.
Page 8:
يَدْخُلُ الكَعْكُ إِلَى الفُرْنِ. الرّائِحَةُ جَمِيلَةٌ!
The cookies go into the oven. The smell is wonderful!
Page 9:
تَأْكُلُ هَنا الكَعْكَ مَعَ تِيتَة. لَذِيذٌ جِدّاً!
Hana eats the cookies with Teta. So delicious!
What did Hana and Teta make together?
They made delicious cookies together.
What fell on the floor?
The flour fell and made a big white mess.
Why did Teta laugh?
The flour made Hana's face white like snow.
A Trip to the Sea. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
في يَوْمِ الجُمُعَةِ، تَذْهَبُ العائِلَةُ إِلَى البَحْرِ.
On Friday, the family goes to the sea.
البَحْرُ كَبِيرٌ وَأَزْرَقُ وَجَمِيلٌ.
The sea is big, blue, and beautiful.
تَبْنِي هَنا بَيْتاً مِنَ الرَّمْلِ.
Hana builds a house out of sand.
تَبْحَثُ هَنا عَنْ أَصْدافٍ صَغِيرَةٍ.
Hana looks for little seashells.
فَجْأَةً، تَأْتِي مَوْجَةٌ كَبِيرَةٌ!
Suddenly, a big wave comes!
أَيْنَ بَيْتُ الرَّمْلِ؟ هَنا حَزِينَةٌ.
Where is the sand house? Hana is sad.
يَقُولُ بابا: هَيّا نَبْنِي بَيْتاً أَكْبَرَ مَعاً!
Baba says: let's build a bigger house together!
يَبْنِي بابا وَهَنا قَصْراً كَبِيراً مِنَ الرَّمْلِ.
Baba and Hana build a big sandcastle.
تَنْظُرُ هَنا إِلَى الشَّمْسِ فَوْقَ البَحْرِ. يَوْمٌ سَعِيدٌ!
Hana looks at the sun over the sea. What a happy day!
Where did the family go on Friday?
The family went to the sea on Friday.
What happened to Hana's sand house?
A big wave came and washed the sand house away.
What did Hana and Baba build together?
They built a big sandcastle together, even better than before.
The New Friend. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
في المَدْرَسَةِ، تَجْلِسُ بِنْتٌ جَدِيدَةٌ وَحْدَها.
At school, a new girl sits alone.
البِنْتُ الجَدِيدَةُ تَبْدُو حَزِينَةً وَخائِفَةً.
The new girl looks sad and scared.
تَذْهَبُ هَنا إِلَيْها وَتَقُولُ: مَرْحَباً!
Hana goes to her and says: hello!
تَقُولُ البِنْتُ: اسْمِي لَيْلَى. أَنا جَدِيدَةٌ هُنا.
The girl says: my name is Layla. I am new here.
تُشارِكُ هَنا لَيْلَى تُفّاحَتَها الحَمْراءَ.
Hana shares her red apple with Layla.
في الاسْتِراحَةِ، تَلْعَبانِ بِالكُرَةِ مَعاً.
At break time, they play ball together.
تَضْحَكُ لَيْلَى. هِيَ لَيْسَتْ حَزِينَةً الآنَ.
Layla laughs. She is not sad anymore.
بَعْدَ المَدْرَسَةِ، تَمْشِيانِ إِلَى البَيْتِ مَعاً.
After school, they walk home together.
الآنَ عِنْدَ هَنا صَدِيقَةٌ جَدِيدَةٌ. الصَّداقَةُ جَمِيلَةٌ!
Now Hana has a new friend. Friendship is beautiful!
Why was the new girl sitting alone?
She was new at the school and felt a little scared.
The new girl said her name was Layla.
What did Hana share with Layla?
Hana shared her red apple with her new friend.
The Football Match. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
اليَوْمَ مُباراةُ كُرَةِ القَدَمِ في المَدْرَسَةِ.
Today is the football match at school.
تَلْبَسُ هَنا حِذاءَها الرِّياضِيَّ الجَدِيدَ.
Hana puts on her new sports shoes.
تَجْرِي هَنا وَراءَ الكُرَةِ بِسُرْعَةٍ.
Hana runs after the ball quickly.
الفَرِيقُ الآخَرُ يُسَجِّلُ هَدَفاً. واحِد - صِفْر!
The other team scores a goal. One to zero!
تَقُولُ هَنا لِفَرِيقِها: هَيّا نُحاوِلُ مَعاً!
Hana says to her team: let's try together!
يَلْعَبُ الفَرِيقُ مَعاً وَيُمَرِّرُ الكُرَةَ.
The team plays together and passes the ball.
تُسَجِّلُ هَنا هَدَفاً جَمِيلاً! واحِد - واحِد!
Hana scores a beautiful goal! One to one!
في النِّهايَةِ، تُسَجِّلُ لَيْلَى هَدَفَ الفَوْزِ!
At the end, Layla scores the winning goal!
يَفْرَحُ الفَرِيقُ. اللَّعِبُ مَعاً أَجْمَلُ شَيْءٍ!
The team celebrates. Playing together is the best thing!
What was the score after the other team scored?
The other team scored first, so it was one to zero.
What did Hana tell her team?
Hana told her team to keep trying together.
Who scored the winning goal?
Layla scored the winning goal at the end.
The Ramadan Lantern. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
يَبْدَأُ شَهْرُ رَمَضانَ اللَّيْلَةَ.
The month of Ramadan begins tonight.
تُرِيدُ هَنا فانُوساً جَمِيلاً مِثْلَ كُلِّ سَنَةٍ.
Hana wants a beautiful lantern, like every year.
تَذْهَبُ هَنا مَعَ بابا إِلَى السُّوقِ.
Hana goes with Baba to the market.
الفَوانِيسُ كُلُّها مُباعَةٌ! هَنا حَزِينَةٌ.
All the lanterns are sold! Hana is sad.
يَقُولُ جِدُّو: نَصْنَعُ فانُوساً بِأَيْدِينا!
Geddo says: we will make a lantern with our own hands!
تَقُصُّ هَنا وَرَقاً أَحْمَرَ وَأَصْفَرَ.
Hana cuts red and yellow paper.
يَضَعُ جِدُّو مِصْباحاً صَغِيراً في الفانُوسِ.
Geddo puts a little light inside the lantern.
الفانُوسُ يُضِيءُ! هُوَ أَجْمَلُ فانُوسٍ في الشّارِعِ.
The lantern lights up! It is the most beautiful lantern on the street.
تُغَنِّي هَنا مَعَ العائِلَةِ. لَيْلَةٌ سَعِيدَةٌ!
Hana sings with the family. What a happy night!
Why was Hana sad at the market?
Every lantern in the market was already sold.
Who had the idea to make a lantern at home?
Geddo said they could make one with their own hands.
What colors of paper did Hana cut?
She cut red and yellow paper for the lantern.
The Magic Kite. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
تَجِدُ هَنا طائِرَةً وَرَقِيَّةً قَدِيمَةً في البَيْتِ.
Hana finds an old kite in the house.
تَرْسُمُ هَنا عَلَيْها شَمْساً وَنُجُوماً صَفْراءَ.
Hana draws a sun and yellow stars on it.
في الحَدِيقَةِ، تَطِيرُ الطّائِرَةُ عالِياً.
In the garden, the kite flies high.
فَجْأَةً، تَلْمَعُ الطّائِرَةُ! إِنَّها طائِرَةٌ سِحْرِيَّةٌ!
Suddenly, the kite sparkles! It is a magic kite!
تَطِيرُ هَنا مَعَ الطّائِرَةِ فَوْقَ البُيُوتِ.
Hana flies with the kite over the houses.
تَرَى البَحْرَ الأَزْرَقَ وَالمَراكِبَ الصَّغِيرَةَ.
She sees the blue sea and the little boats.
تَرَى المَدْرَسَةَ وَالحَدِيقَةَ وَالشَّجَرَةَ الكَبِيرَةَ.
She sees the school, the garden, and the big tree.
تَنْزِلُ الطّائِرَةُ بِهُدُوءٍ في الحَدِيقَةِ.
The kite lands gently in the garden.
تَفْتَحُ هَنا عَيْنَيْها تَحْتَ الشَّجَرَةِ... هَلْ كانَ حُلْماً؟
Hana opens her eyes under the tree... was it a dream?
Where did Hana find the old kite?
She found the old kite in the house.
What did Hana see when she flew with the kite?
She saw the blue sea, little boats, her school, and the big tree.
How does the story end?
She opens her eyes under the tree, so maybe it was all a dream!
A Visit to Egypt. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
في الصَّيْفِ، سافَرَتْ هَنا إِلَى مِصْرَ.
In the summer, Hana traveled to Egypt.
اسْتَقْبَلَها جِدُّو وَتِيتَة في المَطارِ.
Geddo and Teta welcomed her at the airport.
ذَهَبَتِ العائِلَةُ إِلَى بَيْتِ جِدُّو في القاهِرَةِ.
The family went to Geddo's house in Cairo.
طَبَخَتْ تِيتَة طَعاماً مِصْرِيّاً لَذِيذاً.
Teta cooked delicious Egyptian food.
في اليَوْمِ الثّانِي، رَكِبَتْ هَنا جَمَلاً كَبِيراً.
On the second day, Hana rode a big camel.
رَأَتْ هَنا الأَهْراماتِ. ما أَكْبَرَها!
Hana saw the pyramids. How big they are!
فَجْأَةً، لَمْ تَجِدْ هَنا قُبَّعَتَها الحَمْراءَ!
Suddenly, Hana could not find her red hat!
أَخَذَتِ الرِّيحُ القُبَّعَةَ بَعِيداً في الرَّمْلِ.
The wind took the hat far away in the sand.
جَرَى الجَمَلُ وَأَمْسَكَ القُبَّعَةَ بِفَمِهِ!
The camel ran and caught the hat with its mouth!
Page 10:
ضَحِكَ الجَمِيعُ. الجَمَلُ صَدِيقٌ ذَكِيٌّ!
Everyone laughed. The camel is a clever friend!
Page 11:
في آخِرِ يَوْمٍ، رَكِبَتِ العائِلَةُ مَرْكَباً في نَهْرِ النِّيلِ.
On the last day, the family rode a boat on the Nile River.
Page 12:
قالَتْ هَنا: أُحِبُّ مِصْرَ! سَأَعُودُ كُلَّ صَيْفٍ.
Hana said: I love Egypt! I will come back every summer.
Who welcomed Hana at the airport?
Geddo and Teta were waiting for her at the airport.
What took Hana's red hat away?
The wind blew her red hat far away in the sand.
Who caught the hat?
The clever camel ran and caught the hat with its mouth.
The School Trip. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
يَوْمَ الخَمِيسِ، ذَهَبَتِ المَدْرَسَةُ في رِحْلَةٍ.
On Thursday, the school went on a trip.
حَمَلَتْ هَنا حَقِيبَتَها وَطَعامَها وَماءَها.
Hana carried her bag, her food, and her water.
وَصَلَتِ الحافِلَةُ إِلَى حَدِيقَةِ الحَيَواناتِ.
The bus arrived at the zoo.
رَأَتْ هَنا القُرُودَ تَلْعَبُ عَلَى الشَّجَرَةِ.
Hana saw the monkeys playing in the tree.
الفِيلُ الكَبِيرُ شَرِبَ الماءَ بِخُرْطُومِهِ.
The big elephant drank water with its trunk.
فَجْأَةً... أَيْنَ لَيْلَى؟ لَمْ تَجِدْها هَنا!
Suddenly... where is Layla? Hana could not find her!
بَحَثَتْ هَنا عِنْدَ القُرُودِ وَعِنْدَ الفِيلِ.
Hana looked by the monkeys and by the elephant.
أَخْبَرَتْ هَنا المُعَلِّمَةَ بِسُرْعَةٍ.
Hana quickly told the teacher.
وَجَدُوا لَيْلَى أَمامَ بَيْتِ البَطارِيقِ تَضْحَكُ.
They found Layla in front of the penguin house, laughing.
قالَتْ لَيْلَى: البَطارِيقُ تَمْشِي مِثْلِي!
Layla said: the penguins walk just like me!
أَكَلَ الأَصْدِقاءُ الطَّعامَ تَحْتَ شَجَرَةٍ كَبِيرَةٍ.
The friends ate their food under a big tree.
عادَتْ هَنا إِلَى البَيْتِ سَعِيدَةً وَمُتْعَبَةً.
Hana went back home happy and tired.
Where did the school bus go?
The bus took the school to the zoo.
Who got lost for a little while?
Layla disappeared for a little while and everyone looked for her.
Where did they find Layla?
She was watching the penguins and laughing at how they walk.
The Birthday Cake Surprise. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
غَداً عِيدُ مِيلادِ ماما.
Tomorrow is Mama's birthday.
قالَتْ هَنا لِبابا: نَصْنَعُ كَعْكَةً... سِرّاً!
Hana said to Baba: let's make a cake... secretly!
خَلَطَتْ هَنا الدَّقِيقَ وَالسُّكَّرَ وَالبَيْضَ.
Hana mixed the flour, the sugar, and the eggs.
أَضافَ بابا الشُّوكُولاتَةَ. رائِحَةٌ جَمِيلَةٌ!
Baba added the chocolate. What a lovely smell!
وَضَعا الكَعْكَةَ في الفُرْنِ وَانْتَظَرا.
They put the cake in the oven and waited.
يا إِلَهِي! نَسِيا الكَعْكَةَ! دُخانٌ أَسْوَدُ!
Oh no! They forgot the cake! Black smoke!
الكَعْكَةُ مُحْتَرِقَةٌ. جَلَسَتْ هَنا حَزِينَةً.
The cake is burnt. Hana sat down, sad.
حَتَّى القِطَّةُ هَرَبَتْ مِنَ الرّائِحَةِ!
Even the cat ran away from the smell!
قالَ بابا: عِنْدِي فِكْرَةٌ! نَصْنَعُ كَعْكَةً بِلا فُرْنٍ.
Baba said: I have an idea! We will make a cake without an oven.
وَضَعا بَسْكَوِيتاً وَكِرِيمَةً وَفَراوِلَةً حَمْراءَ.
They put biscuits, cream, and red strawberries.
في الصَّباحِ، رَأَتْ ماما الكَعْكَةَ وَفَرِحَتْ كَثِيراً.
In the morning, Mama saw the cake and was very happy.
قالَتْ ماما: أَجْمَلُ كَعْكَةٍ في العالَمِ! وَضَحِكَ الجَمِيعُ.
Mama said: the most beautiful cake in the world! And everyone laughed.
Whose birthday was it?
Hana and Baba made a secret cake for Mama's birthday.
What went wrong with the first cake?
They forgot the cake in the oven and it burnt with black smoke.
How did they fix the problem?
Baba had the idea to make a cake without an oven, using biscuits, cream, and strawberries.
The Camel Ride. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
هَنا تَذْهَبُ إِلى الصَّحْراءِ مَعَ بابا.
Hana goes to the desert with Baba.
تَرى هَنا جَمَلًا كَبيرًا وَجَميلًا.
Hana sees a big, beautiful camel.
الجَمَلُ عالٍ جِدًّا! هَنا خائِفَةٌ.
The camel is very tall! Hana is scared.
بابا يُساعِدُ هَنا. هِيَ تَرْكَبُ الجَمَلَ.
Baba helps Hana. She rides the camel.
الجَمَلُ يَمْشي. هَنا سَعيدَةٌ جِدًّا!
The camel walks. Hana is very happy!
الجَمَلُ يَأْكُلُ قُبَّعَةَ بابا! الجَميعُ يَضْحَكُ.
The camel eats Baba's hat! Everyone laughs.
Where does Hana go with Baba?
Hana goes to the desert with Baba, and that is where she sees the camel.
Why is Hana scared at first?
The camel is very tall, so Hana feels scared before she rides it.
What does the camel eat at the end?
The funny camel eats Baba's hat, and everyone laughs.
The Moon at Night. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
اللَّيْلُ جَميلٌ. هَنا تَنْظُرُ إِلى السَّماءِ.
The night is beautiful. Hana looks at the sky.
القَمَرُ كَبيرٌ وَجَميلٌ اللَّيْلَةَ.
The moon is big and beautiful tonight.
سَحابَةٌ كَبيرَةٌ تُغَطّي القَمَرَ. هَنا حَزينَةٌ.
A big cloud covers the moon. Hana is sad.
هَنا تَنْتَظِرُ مَعَ تيتة في الشُّرْفَةِ.
Hana waits with Teta on the balcony.
السَّحابَةُ تَذْهَبُ! القَمَرُ يَعودُ.
The cloud goes away! The moon comes back.
هَنا تَنامُ وَالقَمَرُ يَنْظُرُ إِلَيْها.
Hana sleeps, and the moon watches over her.
What does Hana look at?
Hana looks at the beautiful night sky to see the moon.
What covers the moon?
A big cloud covers the moon, and Hana feels sad.
Who waits with Hana on the balcony?
Teta waits with Hana on the balcony until the cloud goes away.
The Little Goldfish. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
هَنا عِنْدَها سَمَكَةٌ صَغيرَةٌ وَجَميلَةٌ.
Hana has a small, beautiful fish.
كُلَّ يَوْمٍ تُطْعِمُ هَنا السَّمَكَةَ.
Every day Hana feeds the fish.
اليَوْمَ السَّمَكَةُ لا تَأْكُلُ. هَنا حَزينَةٌ.
Today the fish does not eat. Hana is sad.
ماما تَقولُ: «الماءُ لَيْسَ نَظيفًا».
Mama says the water is not clean.
هَنا تُنَظِّفُ الحَوْضَ بِماءٍ جَديدٍ.
Hana cleans the tank with fresh new water.
السَّمَكَةُ تَأْكُلُ وَتَرْقُصُ في الماءِ!
The fish eats and dances in the water!
What pet does Hana have?
Hana has a small, beautiful fish that she feeds every day.
Why does the fish stop eating?
Mama explains that the water is not clean, so the fish will not eat.
What does the fish do at the end?
After Hana cleans the tank, the happy fish eats and dances in the water.
The Butterfly in the Garden. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
هَنا تَلْعَبُ في الحَديقَةِ الجَميلَةِ.
Hana plays in the beautiful garden.
تَرى فَراشَةً صَغيرَةً عَلى الوَرْدَةِ.
She sees a small butterfly on the rose.
هَنا تَجْري خَلْفَ الفَراشَةِ.
Hana runs after the butterfly.
الفَراشَةُ تَطيرُ بَعيدًا. هَنا حَزينَةٌ.
The butterfly flies far away. Hana is sad.
هَنا تَجْلِسُ بِهُدوءٍ وَلا تَتَحَرَّكُ.
Hana sits quietly and does not move.
الفَراشَةُ تَقِفُ عَلى يَدِها! يا لَلْجَمالِ!
The butterfly lands on her hand! How beautiful!
Where does Hana play?
Hana plays in the beautiful garden, where she sees the butterfly.
What does Hana see on the rose?
A small butterfly is sitting on the rose in the garden.
How does Hana get the butterfly to come to her?
When Hana sits quietly without moving, the butterfly lands on her hand.
The Lost Tooth. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
سِنُّ هَنا يَتَحَرَّكُ!
Hana's tooth is wiggling!
هَنا تَأْكُلُ تُفّاحَةً كَبيرَةً.
Hana eats a big apple.
السِّنُّ يَقَعُ! لكِنْ أَيْنَ هُوَ؟
The tooth falls out! But where is it?
هَنا تَبْحَثُ عَنِ السِّنِّ في كُلِّ مَكانٍ.
Hana looks for the tooth everywhere.
السِّنُّ في التُّفّاحَةِ! الجَميعُ يَضْحَكُ.
The tooth is in the apple! Everyone laughs.
ماما تَقولُ: «الآنَ أَنْتِ كَبيرَةٌ يا هَنا!».
Mama says that Hana is a big girl now!
What is wiggling at the start of the story?
Hana's tooth is wiggling, ready to fall out.
Where does Hana find her tooth?
The tooth fell out into the big apple she was eating!
What does Mama say at the end?
Mama tells Hana that losing a tooth means she is a big girl now.
The Ice-Cream Seller. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
اليَوْمُ حارٌّ جِدًّا. الشَّمْسُ كَبيرَةٌ.
Today is very hot. The sun is big.
هَنا تَسْمَعُ جَرَسَ بائِعِ المُثَلَّجاتِ.
Hana hears the ice-cream seller's bell.
هَنا تَجْري إِلى الشّارِعِ بِسُرْعَةٍ.
Hana runs quickly to the street.
لا نُقودَ مَعَ هَنا! هِيَ حَزينَةٌ.
Hana has no money! She is sad.
البائِعُ يَبْتَسِمُ: «ساعِديني وَخُذي واحِدَةً».
The seller smiles and says she can help him and take one.
هَنا تُساعِدُ البائِعَ وَتَأْكُلُ مُثَلَّجاتِ الفَراوِلَةِ!
Hana helps the seller and eats a strawberry ice cream!
What is the weather like in this story?
It is a very hot day, perfect for ice cream.
Why is Hana sad at first?
Hana runs to the street but she has no money for ice cream.
What flavour of ice cream does Hana eat?
After helping the kind seller, Hana enjoys a strawberry ice cream.
The Little Lamb. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
في بَيْتِ جَدّو خَروفٌ صَغيرٌ وَأَبْيَضُ.
At Geddo's house there is a small white lamb.
هَنا تُحِبُّ الخَروفَ الصَّغيرَ كَثيرًا.
Hana loves the little lamb very much.
البابُ مَفْتوحٌ! الخَروفُ لَيْسَ هُنا!
The door is open! The lamb is not here!
هَنا وَجَدّو يَبْحَثانِ في الحَديقَةِ.
Hana and Geddo search in the garden.
الخَروفُ في المَطْبَخِ يَأْكُلُ الخَسَّ!
The lamb is in the kitchen, eating the lettuce!
تيتة تَضْحَكُ: «هذا خَروفٌ جَوْعانُ!».
Teta laughs and says that is one hungry lamb!
What colour is the little lamb?
Geddo's little lamb is small and white.
Where do they find the lamb?
The lamb sneaked into the kitchen through the open door.
What is the lamb eating?
The hungry lamb is eating the lettuce in the kitchen.
The School Library Book. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
هَنا تَذْهَبُ إِلى مَكْتَبَةِ المَدْرَسَةِ.
Hana goes to the school library.
تَجِدُ كِتابًا جَميلًا عَنِ القِطَطِ.
She finds a beautiful book about cats.
تَأْخُذُ هَنا الكِتابَ إِلى البَيْتِ.
Hana takes the book home.
بَعْدَ أُسْبوعٍ، لا تَجِدُ الكِتابَ!
After a week, she cannot find the book!
هَنا تَبْحَثُ... الكِتابُ تَحْتَ السَّريرِ!
Hana searches everywhere... the book is under the bed!
تُعيدُ هَنا الكِتابَ، وَالمُعَلِّمَةُ سَعيدَةٌ.
Hana returns the book, and the teacher is happy.
What is the library book about?
Hana finds a beautiful book about cats in the school library.
Where does Hana find the lost book?
After searching everywhere, Hana finds the book under her bed.
How does the teacher feel when Hana returns the book?
The teacher is happy because Hana brought the book back.
The Sandcastle Competition. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
اليَوْمَ مُسابَقَةٌ كَبيرَةٌ عَلى الشّاطِئِ.
Today there is a big competition at the beach.
كُلُّ طِفْلٍ يَبْني قَصْرًا مِنَ الرَّمْلِ.
Every child builds a castle out of sand.
هَنا وَلَيْلى تَعْمَلانِ مَعًا.
Hana and Layla work together.
هَنا تَمْلَأُ الدَّلْوَ، وَلَيْلى تَبْني.
Hana fills the bucket, and Layla builds.
مَوْجَةٌ كَبيرَةٌ تَأْتي! يا لَلْمُصيبَةِ!
A big wave comes! Oh no!
نِصْفُ القَصْرِ يَخْتَفي في الماءِ.
Half of the castle disappears into the water.
لكِنَّ البِنْتَيْنِ لا تَسْتَسْلِمانِ. تَبْنيانِ بِسُرْعَةٍ.
But the two girls do not give up. They build quickly.
تَضَعانِ أَصْدافًا جَميلَةً عَلى القَصْرِ.
They put beautiful shells on the castle.
القَصْرُ يَفوزُ بِالجائِزَةِ الأولى!
The castle wins first prize!
Where is the competition?
The big sandcastle competition is at the beach.
What ruins half of the castle?
A big wave comes and washes away half of the castle.
What do the girls put on the castle?
They decorate the castle with beautiful shells, and it wins first prize.
Mama's Famous Dish. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
اليَوْمَ تَطْبُخُ ماما الكُشَري المِصْرِيَّ.
Today Mama is cooking Egypt's famous rice and lentil dish.
هَنا تَقولُ: «أَنا أُساعِدُكِ يا ماما!».
Hana says she will help Mama!
هَنا تَغْسِلُ الأَرُزَّ بِالماءِ.
Hana washes the rice with water.
ماما تَقْلي البَصَلَ. رائِحَتُهُ جَميلَةٌ!
Mama fries the onions. They smell wonderful!
المَعْكَرونَةُ وَالعَدَسُ في القِدْرِ الكَبيرِ.
The pasta and the lentils go into the big pot.
هَنا تُمْسِكُ السُّكَّرَ! «قِفي! هذا لَيْسَ مِلْحًا!».
Hana grabs the sugar! Mama shouts to stop, that is not salt!
ماما تَضْحَكُ وَتُعْطيها المِلْحَ الصَّحيحَ.
Mama laughs and gives her the right salt.
هَنا تَسْكُبُ الصَّلْصَةَ الحَمْراءَ فَوْقَ الطَّبَقِ.
Hana pours the red sauce over the dish.
بابا يَأْكُلُ وَيَقولُ: «أَلَذُّ طَبَقٍ في مِصْرَ!».
Baba eats and says it is the tastiest dish in Egypt!
What does Hana wash for the dish?
Hana helps Mama by washing the rice with water.
What does Hana almost put in the pot by mistake?
Hana grabs the sugar by mistake, but Mama stops her just in time.
What does Baba say about the dish?
Baba loves it and says it is the tastiest dish in Egypt.
The First Snow. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
هَنا تُسافِرُ مَعَ العائِلَةِ في الشِّتاءِ.
Hana travels with her family in the winter.
الجَوُّ بارِدٌ جِدًّا هُنا!
The weather is very cold here!
في الصَّباحِ، تَنْظُرُ هَنا مِنَ النّافِذَةِ.
In the morning, Hana looks out of the window.
كُلُّ شَيْءٍ أَبْيَضُ! هذا هُوَ الثَّلْجُ!
Everything is white! This is snow!
تَلْبَسُ هَنا مِعْطَفًا وَقُفّازَيْنِ.
Hana puts on a coat and gloves.
هَنا وَبابا يَبْنيانِ رَجُلَ ثَلْجٍ.
Hana and Baba build a snowman.
لا جَزَرَ لِلْأَنْفِ! هَنا حَزينَةٌ.
There is no carrot for the nose! Hana is sad.
بابا يَضَعُ مَوْزَةً مَكانَ الأَنْفِ!
Baba puts a banana where the nose should be!
رَجُلُ الثَّلْجِ مُضْحِكٌ! الجَميعُ يَضْحَكُ وَيُصَوِّرُ.
The snowman looks so funny! Everyone laughs and takes photos.
What does Hana see from the window in the morning?
Hana looks out and sees everything covered in white snow for the first time.
What do Hana and Baba build?
Hana and Baba build a snowman together in the snow.
What do they use for the snowman's nose?
There is no carrot, so Baba uses a banana, and it looks very funny.
Eid Morning. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
غَدًا العيدُ! هَنا سَعيدَةٌ جِدًّا.
Tomorrow is Eid! Hana is very happy.
ماما تَشْتَري لِهَنا فُسْتانًا جَديدًا.
Mama buys Hana a new dress.
في الصَّباحِ، تَسْتَيْقِظُ هَنا مُبَكِّرًا.
In the morning, Hana wakes up early.
تَذْهَبُ العائِلَةُ إِلى صَلاةِ العيدِ.
The family goes to the Eid prayer.
بَعْدَ الصَّلاةِ، حَلْوى وَكَعْكٌ لَذيذٌ.
After the prayer, there are sweets and delicious cookies.
لكِنْ أَيْنَ جَدّو؟ هَنا لا تَراهُ.
But where is Geddo? Hana cannot see him.
الجَرَسُ يَرِنُّ. هَنا تَفْتَحُ البابَ.
The doorbell rings. Hana opens the door.
جَدّو هُنا! وَمَعَهُ هَدِيَّةٌ كَبيرَةٌ.
Geddo is here! And he has a big present.
العيدِيَّةُ وَحُضْنٌ كَبيرٌ! أَجْمَلُ عيدٍ!
Eid money and a big hug! The best Eid ever!
What does Mama buy for Hana?
Mama buys Hana a beautiful new dress to wear on Eid.
Who can Hana not find in the morning?
Hana looks around and cannot see Geddo anywhere.
What does Geddo bring when he arrives?
Geddo arrives at the door with a big present, Eid money, and a big hug.
The Talking Parrot. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
في بَيْتِ تيتة بَبَّغاءٌ أَخْضَرُ.
At Teta's house there is a green parrot.
البَبَّغاءُ يَتَكَلَّمُ! يَقولُ: «صَباحُ الخَيْرِ!».
The parrot talks! It says good morning!
هَنا تُعَلِّمُ البَبَّغاءَ كَلِماتٍ جَديدَةً.
Hana teaches the parrot new words.
تيتة تَبْحَثُ عَنِ البَسْكَويتِ: «أَيْنَ البَسْكَويتُ؟».
Teta is looking for the biscuits and asks where they are.
البَبَّغاءُ يَصيحُ: «القِطُّ أَخَذَها! القِطُّ أَخَذَها!».
The parrot shouts that the cat took them!
لكِنَّ القِطَّ نائِمٌ عَلى الكُرْسِيِّ.
But the cat is fast asleep on the chair.
هَنا تَنْظُرُ إِلى قَفَصِ البَبَّغاءِ.
Hana looks at the parrot's cage.
البَسْكَويتُ داخِلَ القَفَصِ! البَبَّغاءُ هُوَ السّارِقُ!
The biscuits are inside the cage! The parrot is the thief!
البَبَّغاءُ يَقولُ: «أَنا آسِفٌ! أَنا آسِفٌ!».
The parrot says it is sorry, it is sorry!
What colour is the parrot?
Teta has a green parrot that can talk.
Who does the parrot say took the biscuits?
The parrot blames the cat, but the cat is asleep on the chair.
Who really took the biscuits?
Hana finds the biscuits inside the cage, so the parrot is the real thief!
The Bicycle Lesson. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
هَنا عِنْدَها دَرّاجَةٌ جَديدَةٌ وَحَمْراءُ.
Hana has a new red bicycle.
لكِنَّها لا تَعْرِفُ الرُّكوبَ بَعْدُ.
But she does not know how to ride it yet.
بابا يَقولُ: «أَنا أُمْسِكُ الدَّرّاجَةَ، لا تَخافي».
Baba says he will hold the bicycle, so do not be afraid.
هَنا تَرْكَبُ، وَبابا يَجْري خَلْفَها.
Hana rides, and Baba runs behind her.
هَنا تَقَعُ عَلى العُشْبِ. آه!
Hana falls onto the grass. Ouch!
هَنا حَزينَةٌ وَتُريدُ أَنْ تَتَوَقَّفَ.
Hana is sad and wants to stop.
بابا يَقولُ: «مَرَّةً أَخيرَةً، أَنْتِ قَوِيَّةٌ!».
Baba says one more try, because she is strong!
هَنا تَرْكَبُ وَحْدَها! بابا لا يُمْسِكُ!
Hana rides all by herself! Baba is not holding on!
«أَنا أَرْكَبُ! أَنا أَرْكَبُ!» تَصيحُ هَنا بِفَرَحٍ.
I am riding, I am riding, shouts Hana with joy!
What colour is Hana's new bicycle?
Hana has a brand new red bicycle.
Where does Hana fall?
Hana falls onto the soft grass while learning to ride.
What does Baba say after Hana falls?
Baba encourages Hana to try one more time because she is strong, and she rides by herself!
The Swimming Lesson. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
اليَوْمَ أَوَّلُ دَرْسِ سِباحَةٍ لِهَنا.
Today is Hana's first swimming lesson.
المَسْبَحُ كَبيرٌ، وَهَنا خائِفَةٌ قَليلًا.
The pool is big, and Hana is a little scared.
المُدَرِّبَةُ تَقولُ: «الماءُ صَديقُكِ يا هَنا».
The coach says the water is her friend.
هَنا تَجْلِسُ وَتُحَرِّكُ رِجْلَيْها في الماءِ.
Hana sits and kicks her legs in the water.
ثُمَّ تَنْزِلُ إِلى الماءِ بِبُطْءٍ.
Then she goes into the water slowly.
الماءُ في أَنْفِها! هَنا لا تُحِبُّ هذا.
Water goes up her nose! Hana does not like that.
المُدَرِّبَةُ تُعَلِّمُها أَنْ تَنْفُخَ فُقاعاتٍ.
The coach teaches her to blow bubbles.
بَعْدَ قَليلٍ، هَنا تَعومُ مِثْلَ سَمَكَةٍ صَغيرَةٍ!
Soon, Hana is floating like a little fish!
ماما تُصَفِّقُ: «أَنْتِ سَمَكَتي الصَّغيرَةُ!».
Mama claps and calls her my little fish!
How does Hana feel at her first swimming lesson?
The pool is big, so Hana feels a little scared at first.
What goes up Hana's nose?
Water goes up her nose, and she does not like that at all.
What does the coach teach Hana to do?
The coach teaches her to blow bubbles, and soon she floats like a little fish.
Geddo's Old Radio. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
عِنْدَ جَدّو راديو قَديمٌ وَبُنِّيٌّ.
Geddo has an old brown radio.
كُلَّ مَساءٍ يَسْمَعُ جَدّو الأَغاني القَديمَةَ.
Every evening Geddo listens to the old songs.
اليَوْمَ الرّاديو لا يَعْمَلُ!
Today the radio does not work!
جَدّو حَزينٌ جِدًّا. هَنا تُريدُ أَنْ تُساعِدَ.
Geddo is very sad. Hana wants to help.
هَنا تَنْظُرُ خَلْفَ الرّاديو الكَبيرِ.
Hana looks behind the big radio.
السِّلْكُ لَيْسَ في الكَهْرَباءِ!
The wire is not plugged in!
القِطُّ لَعِبَ بِالسِّلْكِ في اللَّيْلِ!
The cat played with the wire in the night!
هَنا تُعيدُ السِّلْكَ، وَالأَغاني تَعودُ.
Hana plugs the wire back in, and the songs come back.
جَدّو وَهَنا يَرْقُصانِ عَلى الأَغاني القَديمَةِ!
Geddo and Hana dance to the old songs!
What does Geddo listen to every evening?
Geddo loves listening to the old songs on his radio every evening.
Why did the radio stop working?
Hana looks behind the radio and finds the wire is not plugged in.
Who played with the wire?
The cat played with the wire in the night and pulled it out.
A Picnic by the Nile. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
في يَوْمِ الجُمُعَةِ، ذَهَبَتِ العائِلَةُ إِلى النّيلِ.
On Friday, the family went to the Nile.
حَمَلَتْ ماما سَلَّةً كَبيرَةً مِنَ الطَّعامِ.
Mama carried a big basket of food.
جَلَسوا تَحْتَ شَجَرَةٍ كَبيرَةٍ بِجانِبِ الماءِ.
They sat under a big tree beside the water.
شاهَدَتْ هَنا المَراكِبَ الجَميلَةَ عَلى النّيلِ.
Hana watched the beautiful boats on the Nile.
فَتَحَتْ ماما السَّلَّةَ: سَنْدَويشاتٌ وَفاكِهَةٌ وَعَصيرٌ.
Mama opened the basket: sandwiches, fruit, and juice.
جاءَتْ بَطَّةٌ صَغيرَةٌ إِلى هَنا.
A little duck came up to Hana.
أَعْطَتْ هَنا البَطَّةَ قِطْعَةَ خُبْزٍ.
Hana gave the duck a piece of bread.
فَجْأَةً، جاءَتْ عَشْرُ بَطّاتٍ جَوْعانَةٍ!
Suddenly, ten hungry ducks came!
البَطّاتُ جَرَتْ خَلْفَ هَنا وَالسَّنْدَويشاتِ!
The ducks chased Hana and the sandwiches!
ضَحِكَ بابا كَثيرًا وَساعَدَ هَنا.
Baba laughed a lot and helped Hana.
أَكَلَتِ العائِلَةُ وَشاهَدَتْ غُروبَ الشَّمْسِ.
The family ate and watched the sunset.
قالَتْ هَنا: «أَجْمَلُ يَوْمٍ بِجانِبِ النّيلِ!».
Hana said it was the most beautiful day by the Nile!
The family went for a picnic by the Nile and sat under a big tree.
What did Hana give the little duck?
Hana kindly gave the little duck a piece of bread.
What happened after Hana fed the first duck?
Ten hungry ducks suddenly came and chased Hana and the sandwiches!
The Art Competition. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
أَعْلَنَتِ المُعَلِّمَةُ عَنْ مُسابَقَةِ رَسْمٍ كَبيرَةٍ.
The teacher announced a big art competition.
فَكَّرَتْ هَنا: «ماذا أَرْسُمُ؟».
Hana thought about what she should draw.
رَسَمَتْ لَيْلى قِطَّةً جَميلَةً.
Layla drew a beautiful cat.
قَرَّرَتْ هَنا أَنْ تَرْسُمَ البَحْرَ.
Hana decided to draw the sea.
رَسَمَتْ هَنا كُلَّ يَوْمٍ بَعْدَ المَدْرَسَةِ.
Hana painted every day after school.
قَبْلَ المُسابَقَةِ، وَقَعَ العَصيرُ عَلى الرَّسْمِ!
Before the competition, juice spilled on the painting!
بَكَتْ هَنا. في الرَّسْمِ بُقْعَةٌ بُرْتُقالِيَّةٌ.
Hana cried. There was an orange stain on the painting.
ثُمَّ نَظَرَتْ هَنا إِلى البُقْعَةِ وَفَكَّرَتْ.
Then Hana looked at the stain and thought.
حَوَّلَتْ هَنا البُقْعَةَ إِلى شَمْسٍ جَميلَةٍ!
Hana turned the stain into a beautiful setting sun!
الآنَ في الرَّسْمِ بَحْرٌ وَغُروبٌ ذَهَبِيٌّ.
Now the painting had a sea and a golden sunset.
فازَتْ هَنا بِالمَرْكَزِ الأَوَّلِ!
Hana won first place!
قالَتِ المُعَلِّمَةُ: «أَجْمَلُ غُروبٍ رَأَيْتُهُ!».
The teacher said it was the most beautiful sunset she had ever seen!
What did Hana decide to draw?
Hana decided to draw the sea, while Layla drew a cat.
What spilled on Hana's painting?
Juice spilled on the painting and left an orange stain.
How did Hana fix the stain?
Hana was clever: she turned the orange stain into a golden sunset and won first place!
A Visit to the Pyramids. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
رَكِبَتِ العائِلَةُ السَّيّارَةَ إِلى الجيزَةِ.
The family drove in the car to Giza.
مِنْ بَعيدٍ، رَأَتْ هَنا الأَهْراماتِ الكَبيرَةَ.
From far away, Hana saw the great pyramids.
قالَ بابا: «بَنى المِصْرِيّونَ هذا مِنْ زَمَنٍ بَعيدٍ».
Baba said the Egyptians built this long, long ago.
وَقَفَتْ هَنا كَأَنَّها تَحْمِلُ الهَرَمَ!
Hana stood as if she was holding up the pyramid!
رَكِبَتْ هَنا حِصانًا صَغيرًا مَعَ بابا.
Hana rode a small horse with Baba.
رَأَوْا أَبا الهَوْلِ بِوَجْهِ الإِنْسانِ.
They saw the Sphinx with its human face.
سَأَلَتْ هَنا: «أَيْنَ أَنْفُ أَبي الهَوْلِ؟».
Hana asked where the Sphinx's nose was.
ضَحِكَ جَدّو: «هذا سِرٌّ قَديمٌ جِدًّا!».
Geddo laughed and said that is a very old secret!
كانَ الجَوُّ حارًّا، فَشَرِبوا عَصيرَ قَصَبٍ.
The weather was hot, so they drank sugar-cane juice.
في المَتْحَفِ، رَأَتْ هَنا ذَهَبَ المُلوكِ.
In the museum, Hana saw the gold of the kings.
اِشْتَرَتْ هَنا كِتابًا صَغيرًا عَنِ الفَراعِنَةِ.
Hana bought a little book about the pharaohs.
قالَتْ هَنا: «أَنا فَخورَةٌ لِأَنَّني مِصْرِيَّةٌ!».
Hana said she was proud to be Egyptian!
What did Hana see from far away?
On the drive to Giza, Hana saw the great pyramids from far away.
What is missing from the Sphinx?
Hana noticed the Sphinx has no nose, and Geddo said it is a very old secret.
What did the family drink because it was hot?
The weather was hot, so they drank cool sugar-cane juice.
The Planetarium Trip. Let's read! Listen to each page, follow the Arabic, and read along out loud.
ذَهَبَ الصَّفُّ في رِحْلَةٍ إِلى القُبَّةِ الفَلَكِيَّةِ.
The class went on a trip to the planetarium.
قالَتْ لَيْلى: «ما هِيَ القُبَّةُ الفَلَكِيَّةُ؟».
Layla asked what a planetarium is.
دَخَلوا قاعَةً كَبيرَةً وَمُظْلِمَةً.
They entered a big, dark hall.
خافَتْ لَيْلى مِنَ الظَّلامِ.
Layla was scared of the dark.
أَمْسَكَتْ هَنا بِيَدِ صَديقَتِها.
Hana held her friend's hand.
فَجْأَةً، ظَهَرَتْ آلافُ النُّجومِ فَوْقَهُم!
Suddenly, thousands of stars appeared above them!
قالَتْ لَيْلى: «هذا أَجْمَلُ شَيْءٍ رَأَيْتُهُ!».
Layla said it was the most beautiful thing she had ever seen!
رَأَوْا كَواكِبَ كَبيرَةً وَقَمَرًا قَريبًا.
They saw big planets and a moon that looked very close.
قالَتِ المُعَلِّمَةُ: «الشَّمْسُ نَجْمٌ كَبيرٌ».
The teacher said the sun is a big star.
رَأَوْا نَجْمًا يَجْري في السَّماءِ!
They saw a shooting star race across the sky!
أَغْمَضَتْ هَنا عَيْنَيْها وَتَمَنَّتْ أُمْنِيَةً.
Hana closed her eyes and made a wish.
في اللَّيْلِ، نَظَرَتْ هَنا إِلى النُّجومِ مِنَ النّافِذَةِ.
That night, Hana watched the stars from her window.
Where did the class go on their trip?
The class went on a school trip to the planetarium to see the stars.
Who was scared of the dark hall?
Layla was scared of the dark, so Hana held her hand.
What did the teacher say about the sun?
The teacher explained that the sun is really a big star.
